did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM), a machine learning classifier, to classify skin lesion images (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. benign nevus) based on combined BEMD-Riesz and GLDM texture features extracted from dermoscopy images.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper is explicitly labeled as 'RESEARCH' in the header. It presents original experimental work involving a novel technique (combined BEMD-Riesz and GLDM features) and a quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM) for skin lesion classification. The study includes specific methodology (hair removal, segmentation, feature extraction), a defined dataset (56 dermoscopy images from ISIC), and reports original results (100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity) rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Combined empirical mode decomposition and texture features for skin lesion classification using quadratic support vector machine
brief_summary: This study addresses the classification of Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus benign nevi to aid in early skin cancer diagnosis. The authors proposed a novel method combining bi-dimensional empirical mode decomposition (BEMD) with Riesz transform features and gray-level difference method (GLDM) texture features. The dataset consisted of 56 dermoscopy images from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC), comprising 29 BCC and 27 nevus cases. Pre-processing involved hair removal using DullRazor and segmentation via Otsu thresholding enhanced by discrete wavelet transform. The combined features were classified using a quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM), achieving 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity, outperforming other feature combinations and SVM kernels.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 29 BCC images from the ISIC archive. The study focuses on effectively classifying this malignant lesion.
One of the two classes in the binary classification task. The dataset contained 27 nevus images from the ISIC archive. Used as the benign comparator to BCC.

all_task_types.target: Binary diagnostic category (Basal Cell Carcinoma vs. benign nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the purpose is to 'classify skin lesion images into two classes, namely the malignant Basal cell carcinoma and the benign nevus' using a quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM). The task is binary classification, evidenced by the use of confusion matrix metrics (TP, FP, TN, FN) and performance metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopy images used for skin lesion classification. The study utilized a subset of 56 images from this archive.

use.training: Used for training the quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM) classifier (46 images).
use.testing: Used for testing the classifier (10 images).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F-measure, and ROC curves, with comparisons made based on metric values rather than statistical significance.

ISIC_archive_subset.dataset_description: 56 dermoscopy images (29 Basal Cell Carcinoma, 27 benign nevus); 46 training images, 10 test images
ISIC_archive_subset.metric: Performance metrics for different feature combinations using Quadratic SVM (Q-SVM)
ISIC_archive_subset.notes: The proposed combined BEMD-Riesz and GLDM features with Q-SVM achieved 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity. This outperformed other feature combinations (BEMD, BEMD-Riesz, GLDM) and other SVM kernel functions (linear, cubic, Gaussian). The study notes that the high resemblance between BCC and benign nevus classes makes this result significant.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Combined Bi-dimensional Empirical Mode Decomposition (BEMD) with Riesz transform features and Gray-Level Difference Method (GLDM) texture features

training_details.dataset: ISIC archive subset (56 dermoscopy images: 29 BCC, 27 nevus)
training_details.hardware: Intel-Core i5-2410 M 2.3 GHz processor, 8 GB RAM, MSWin.7

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using DullRazor software to suppress unwanted hairs
Conversion from RGB color planes to grayscale
Segmentation using Otsu's global thresholding technique enhanced by Discrete Wavelet Transform (DWT) to extract low frequency components (contour boundary) and high frequency components (details)
conclusions: The proposed method effectively classifies Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) versus benign nevi using a quadratic support vector machine (Q-SVM) combined with bi-dimensional empirical mode decomposition (BEMD)-Riesz and gray-level difference method (GLDM) features.
The proposed combined feature extraction technique achieved 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the test set of 56 dermoscopy images from the ISIC archive.
The proposed combined BEMD-Riesz and GLDM features outperformed individual feature sets (BEMD only, BEMD-Riesz only, and GLDM only) in terms of classification performance.
Among different SVM kernel functions tested (linear, cubic, Gaussian, and quadratic), the quadratic polynomial kernel (Q-SVM) provided the superior performance for this dataset.
The high visual resemblance between malignant BCC and benign nevus lesions makes the effective classification of these classes a challenging task, which the proposed system addressed successfully.
important_remarks: The study uses an extremely small dataset (56 dermoscopy images: 29 BCC, 27 nevus) with a fixed split of 46 training and 10 test images, which limits statistical power and generalizability.

The reported performance is 100% accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity on the test set, which is unusually high and likely indicative of overfitting given the small sample size and lack of cross-validation or external validation.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the superiority of the proposed method over comparators.
The dataset is drawn from the ISIC archive, but the specific selection criteria for the 56 images are not detailed, raising potential concerns about dataset bias.
Preprocessing relied on DullRazor for hair removal and Otsu thresholding enhanced by DWT; the effectiveness of these steps on diverse skin tones or lesion types is not evaluated.
The study focuses only on binary classification (BCC vs. benign nevus), limiting clinical relevance for broader skin cancer screening which involves multiple classes (e.g., melanoma, SCC).

The authors acknowledge the high visual resemblance between BCC and benign nevi as a challenge, yet claim 100% separation, which warrants skepticism regarding the robustness of the feature extraction.
